To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43591/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of additional funding, allocated to date, for Irish-medium assessment materials, broken down by financial year; and (ii) the proportion of that funding that has been directed towards the development of Irish-language curriculum resources.
answer_text: From 2025-26 school year, interim assessments arrangements are being put in place to identify end of Key Stage pupil performance in literacy and numeracy via paper-based tests. The total three year costs for the assessment arrangements are £2.68 million. All pupils in all Irish-medium (IM) schools in Years 4, 7 and 10 will participate in new Key Stage assessments during each year of the interim arrangements. Pupils in Irish-medium schools will complete two tests at Key Stage 1 (Irish; and Numeracy) and three tests at Key Stage 2 and 3 (Irish, Literacy (English) and Numeracy) to reflect the introduction of English as a formal area of learning in Year 4. The tests are provided by and marked by CCEA. Pupils sat the written assessments for 2025/26 week commencing 9 March 2026. The Department met the costs of substitute cover for the invigilation of the assessment. The written assessments were provided by CCEA, and no additional funding was allocated to the schools.In addition, the CCEA Computer Adaptive Assessment has been further developed to provide a formative assessment tool at pupil level and to inform school self-evaluation. Invest in upscaling and roll-out of CCEA’s Computer Adaptive Assessments have costs of £1.6m. The Irish-medium adaptive Numeracy assessment has been piloted in Irish-medium schools over the last two years. CCEA has also worked with schools and stakeholders to create an assessment framework for a bespoke Irish literacy test, which will now be integrated within the CAA tool infrastructure and, following test development, piloting will begin. It is anticipated that the IM CAA Literacy and Numeracy will be operational across Key Stage 2 and 3 by autumn 2028. Funding allocated to date has been focused on assessment arrangements. Consideration of funding for Irishmedium curriculum resources will be taken forward as part of the advancement of TransformED and the Irishmedium Education Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 42820/22-27, to detail any areas of work currently being progressed through the Joint Health and Education Oversight Group.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43591/22-27, to detail any gaps in language-appropriate curriculum resources for Irish-medium education that have been identified to date through the development of the Irish-medium Education Strategy.
answer_text: Research undertaken in recent years has highlighted a range of pressures across the Irishmedium education (IME) sector, including challenges relating to the availability of languageappropriate curriculum resources.Evidence from a number of published research papers has indicated that teachers in Irishmedium settings often experience a greater requirement to adapt, translate or create curriculum and teaching materials, contributing to increased workload. These issues have been noted in Ensuring Effective Teacher Supply in the Irishmedium Sector (2023), Fair, Shared, Supported: Expectations and Realities for IM Practitioners (2023), and Teacher Workload in the Irishmedium Sector: Evidential Insights (2025).Building on this evidence, the identification of any specific gaps in languageappropriate curriculum resources is currently being further explored through the development of the IrishMedium Education Strategy. This work is being taken forward by the IrishMedium Strategy Working Group, under the Teaching and Learning pillar of the Strategy, which is presently under development.As part of this process, a more detailed and systematic assessment of resource needs across phases and subject areas will be undertaken. Any gaps identified will be fully evidenced to ensure that future actions are informed by a robust evidence base and align with the overall strategic approach to strengthening Irishmedium education.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43240/22-27, to detail (i) any specific capital investment needs for Irish Medium Education identified to date; (ii) any interim measures in place pending the publication of the strategy; and (iii) when final decisions on capital investment will be taken.
answer_text: (i) A number of capital investment needs within the Irishmedium sector have been identified, including additional and replacement accommodation, school relocations from temporary or leased sites, expansion of postprimary provision, and urgent health and safety works. These needs are being considered as part of existing capital arrangements, and will be further considered by overarching actions in the developing Irishmedium Education Strategy, informed by ongoing engagement with the sector.(ii) In advance of the Strategy’s publication, the Department continues to support Irishmedium education through existing capital arrangements. These include the Irishmedium Accommodation Fund, prioritised minor works, progression of approved development proposals, and delivery of essential interim accommodation to address immediate pressures and statutory requirements.(iii) Decisions on future capital investment will be taken in line with the conclusions of the Irishmedium Education Strategy and the funding available at that time. Any additional capital funding will be considered and agreed as the Strategy progresses, taking account of the very challenging budgetary position across the Department and competing capital priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 42822/22-27, to detail (i) how the revised curriculum will ensure representation of authors, playwrights and writers from across the island of Ireland; and (ii) whether any minimum requirements or guidance will be introduced to support this.
answer_text: The revised curriculum is designed to ensure that pupils engage with a broad range of texts drawn from different periods, cultures and literary traditions, and spanning increasing levels of complexity. While the framework remains in draft, it is expected to set out guiding principles for text selection and may include illustrative examples of authors or works. Decisions about precise texts, however, will remain at school level. Implementation will be supported through high-quality teaching resources, professional development, and, where appropriate, clear guidance to support schools in meeting the revised requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the success of the Schools Procurement Pilot Scheme will be measured; (ii) any anticipated cost savings or efficiency gains; and (iii) whether schools will have the option to permanently opt out of Education Authority procurement frameworks.
answer_text: Consultation with a representative group of schools is underway to inform the final design and implementation of the Schools Procurement Pilot Scheme. This engagement will define the detailed operating model and the criteria against which its success will be measured.The pilot will be evaluated against a clear set of qualitative and quantitative measures. These are expected to include, but will not be limited to, the impact on school autonomy and workload, the effectiveness and timeliness of procurement processes, value for money, compliance with public procurement requirements, and feedback from participating schools.Any potential cost savings or efficiency gains will be identified and assessed as part of the pilot. Consideration will be given when comparing alternative procurement approaches with existing Education Authority procurement frameworks to determine whether they deliver measurable financial benefits, improved purchasing outcomes, or administrative efficiencies. Robust baseline data and ongoing monitoring will be used to support this analysis. Evaluation and review mechanisms will be embedded and applied from the outset to ensure the development of a strong and reliable evidence base with a comprehensive evaluation at the conclusion of the pilot that will inform decisions on future procurement arrangements.As part of this overall assessment, consideration will be given to whether schools should have the option to permanently opt out of Education Authority procurement frameworks, taking into account value for money, accountability, and the need to ensure effective and consistent procurement practice across the school system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 42903/22-27, to detail (i) any measurable outcomes achieved to date through the RAISE programme; (ii) how success is being assessed across participating localities; and (iii) any contingency plans in place, should further funding beyond August 2027 not be secured.
answer_text: (i) Locality Reference Groups have been established in all 18 RAISE localities promoting collaboration between schools and community organisations. The RAISE team have supported the Locality Reference Groups to develop a bespoke Strategic Area Plan for each locality, identifying the key issues that are underpinning educational disadvantage within the locality, putting forward proposals on what is needed to address those needs and outlining recommendations on how best to support the educational achievement of children and young people in that area. Following a successful call for Locality Led Projects 65 projects with a value of up to £3.84 million are now beginning delivery across the 18 localities. These will deliver against the key objectives outlined in the RAISE Strategic Area Plans and the outcomes for each project will be evaluated as delivery progresses.  The programme also includes a number of Cross-cutting Initiatives that are complementing the Locality Led Projects, with delivery and evaluation underway for six of these at present.  A further call for projects is live at present.    (ii) The success of the RAISE programme will be assessed at Project level, Locality Level, and Programme level in line with the published Evaluation Framework.  Evaluation will include quantitative and qualitative data on the impact of the programme on the children and young people, families, education professionals, and communities that have benefited from each Project or Initiative.Analysis to inform the evaluation of the Programme is carried out by a Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) statistician, complemented by the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) and an Independent Evaluator. The impact of the Programme will be reviewed against the Strategic Objectives of the Programme.  Following a procurement competition the National Children’s Bureau (NCB) were appointed as the independent evaluator for the Programme, with Queen’s University Belfast acting as a partner.  The NCB will work alongside the ETI and NISRA statisticians to evaluate the RAISE Programme and to determine if the investment targeted at interventions is making a positive impact on children and young people.(iii) Opportunities for future funding are being explored jointly by officials from my Department, the Department of Education and Youth (IE) and the Department for the Taoiseach in line with the usual processes.  Consideration will be given to contingency planning in parallel with these discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43239/22-27,to detail (i) any steps currently being taken to address any gaps in language-appropriate curriculum resources and assessment materials for Irish Medium Education; and (ii) whether any additional funding has been allocated for this purpose.
answer_text: (i) In line with the commitments in TransformED, my Department has invested significantly in improvements across a range of assessment materials, in addition to progressing an Independent Review of Assessment which makes specific longer-term recommendations around pupil assessment in Irish-medium Education (IME) settings.As part of interim arrangements, all pupils in Years 4, 7 and 10 in all IME schools participated in new, low workload, low stakes Key Stage assessments, drafted by IME practitioners, that will provide system-level data on performance in literacy and numeracy.    In addition, the CCEA Computer Adaptive Assessment has been further developed to provide a formative assessment tool at pupil level and to inform school self-evaluation.  The Irish-medium adaptive Numeracy assessment has been piloted in IME schools over the last two years.  CCEA has also worked with schools and stakeholders to create an assessment framework for a bespoke Irish literacy test, which will now be integrated within the CAA tool infrastructure and, following test development, piloting will begin.  It is anticipated that the IM CAA Literacy and Numeracy will be operational across Key Stage 2 and 3 by autumn 2028.In regard to language-appropriate curriculum resources, this issue is actively being considered as part of the development of the IME Strategy.(ii) As outlined above, additional funding has been allocated in regard to assessment materials for IME. Consideration of any future funding for curriculum resources will take place as the IME Strategy is further developed and will be dependent on the resources available to my Department, which continues to face significant and sustained financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagements he had on his recent trip to the United States.
answer_text: I travelled to the United States for a fiveday programme of events over St Patrick’s Week. My schedule in Washington, D.C. included attending the Ireland Funds National Gala, the U.S. Speaker’s Luncheon, a White House reception and events hosted by the Northern Ireland Bureau and the Department for Communities. I also met with Governor Haley Barbour to discuss education reforms and held discussions with representatives from Microsoft on the use of digital and AI technologies in education. Following my engagements in Washington, I travelled to Louisiana to meet representatives from the Department of Education, local school districts and to visit a school in Tangipahoa Parish as well as Norco Elementary School. During the visit, I saw first-hand how Louisiana has developed one of the most comprehensive statewide approaches to improving literacy in the United States. Many of the themes underpinning the reforms in Louisiana, including strong curriculum, clear standards, structured literacy, and highquality teacher development, closely align with the priorities I am advancing in Northern Ireland through our TransformED Education Reform Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of his recent trip to the United States.
answer_text: I travelled to Washington D.C. and Louisiana for a series of meetings to discuss educational reforms and TransformED priorities. The visit presented a valuable opportunity for dialogue on curriculum quality, professional development, intervention design and the effective use of technology to strengthen literacy outcomes. I was accompanied by my Special Adviser and one departmental official. A breakdown of the overall costs is provided below. Travel: £7,539Accommodation: £6,590

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proportion of eligible pupils who receive free school meals.
answer_text: Information provided by the Education Authority (EA) indicates that 57,027 applications were submitted between 1 September 2025 and 31 March 2026. These applications covered a total of 106,346 children, of whom 92,511 met the criteria for entitlement to free school meals.Entitlement may not be awarded for a range of reasons, for example where children are included in applications who do not meet the age criteria or where family income exceeds the threshold.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 40384/22-27, whether percentages were calculated by dividing the number of children with dyslexia by the total number of children who received a statutory assessment in that year within the SEN Register 1 Category of Cognition and Learning.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised:The percentages were calculated using all children who received a statutory assessment within the Academic Year, as all these children had an identified SEN primary need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the current relationship between his Department and higher education institutions in relation to Initial Teacher Education.
answer_text: In October 2025, the Department commissioned Professor Daniel Muijs of Queen’s University Belfast to support the development of a Core Content Framework (CCF) for Initial Teacher Education (ITE) in Northern Ireland. Throughout the course of his work, Professor Muijs proactively engaged with a broad range of stakeholders, including representatives from each of the HEIs, a process which shaped his recommendations.Following receipt of Professor Muijs’ report, the Department then developed a draft Framework for Initial Teacher Education in Northern Ireland. This was recently issued to the Vice Chancellors of Queen’s and Ulster University and the Principals of Stranmillis and St Mary’s University College, inviting further feedback from each of the HEIs. This process is ongoing and responses have been requested by 3 April 2026. My Department has a constructive relationship with the HEIs and regularly engages with all four and their umbrella body, the Universities’ Council for the Education of Teachers in Northern Ireland (UCETNI). This collaborative approach ensures that they remain central to shaping and delivering the TransformED strategy objectives for Initial Teacher Education and wider priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) the RAISE programme; and (ii) the future of the programme.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme is present in 18 localities across Northern Ireland and consists of two key elements, Locality Led Projects and Cross Cutting Initiatives. Following the first call for Locality Led Projects, 65 successful projects are advancing with project delivery having commenced in the majority of these. A second call for Locality Led Projects to address a further 52 objectives in the Strategic Area Plans was launched on 19 March 2026 and will close on 22 April 2026.On 18 March 2026, the Department issued a separate call for proposals to deliver Home School Community Connection projects, aimed at enhancing links between home, school and the community in the 12 RAISE localities that prioritised these objectives in their Strategic Area Plans.  This call will close on 22 April 2026.A series of Cross-Cutting Initiatives that complement Locality Led Projects and provide additional opportunities to tackle key common issues are also being progressed.  These will span RAISE localities and beyond and are at different stages of development. Those currently in the delivery phase are:The Reading with AI Research Project – currently live in 114 schools.The Holiday Revision Support Programme - 60 schools provided revision support during the Halloween 2025 holidays, and over 100 schools providing support over the Easter 2026 holidays.The Online Science of Learning Programme – 200 schools and 800 teachers registered to the Classroom Excellence Programme.Scoil Spreagtha - Officially relaunched on 26 January and currently reaching 104 schools, 931 teachers and more than 20,000 pupils, Collaborative Professional Learning Clusters - Applications from all eligible schools have been received and will be assessed.Alternative Education Models Pilot - Queen’s University, Belfast, has been appointed to take forward the research element of this Cross Cutting Initiative and work is now underway. The two small-scale pilots to test potential approaches are being developed.  The remaining Cross-Cutting Initiatives are in development and will be delivered during the 2026/27 academic year.The National Children’s Bureau and Queen’s University Belfast have been appointed as the RAISE Programme’s Independent Evaluator to examine if the Programme is making a positive impact for children and young people.€20m has been secured from the Shared Island Fund for the RAISE Programme.  This will enable the Department to deliver the Programme and the projects and initiatives flowing from it through until 31 August 2027.  There is a potential for a further two years of funding, but this is dependent on positive evaluation of the first two years of the Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of whether his Department’s childcare policies are contributing to rising childcare costs for parents.
answer_text: There is no evidence to indicate that the childcare policies introduced by my Department as part of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy are contributing to sector-wide increases in childcare costs for parents. Fee increases above the rate of inflation have always been a factor and pre-date policies such as the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) and are not unique to Northern Ireland.Evidence indicates that childcare costs are increasing across all UK jurisdictions. The Coram Childcare Survey 2026 reports increases of 8.4% in Scotland and 10.5% in Wales for 50 hours of nursery for children under two. Data from NICSS providers indicates a 10.1% increase in Northern Ireland for the same cohort.These trends indicate that increases in childcare costs are driven by wider factors affecting the sector, including staffing costs and inflationary pressures, rather than specific individual Departmental policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the the premium afforded to Irish Medium Education under the common funding formula.
answer_text: The Common Funding Formula (CFF) includes two factors that provide additional support for Irishmedium education: one relating to curricular support and another relating to administrative support for Irishmedium units. The operation of these factors is kept under review as part of wider CFF monitoring arrangements. These factors will also be examined further as part of the work to develop an Irishmedium Education Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the Education Committee’s consultation on the right of all pupils to wear trousers, whether he will revise his Department’s statutory guidance to reflect that position.
answer_text: I have not had sight of any report about the Committee’s consultation on the right of all pupils to wear trousers.  Furthermore, I remain of the view that there is no barrier whatsoever in the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Act (Northern Ireland) 2026 or the School Uniforms Guidelines to all pupils wearing trousers as part of their school uniform, should they wish to do so.The guidelines require schools to have full regard to the pupil voice, to the comfort and practicality of uniform requirements, to equality and human rights legislation.  Schools should be given the opportunity to implement the statutory guidelines before further changes are considered to allow an assessment of the effectiveness of the guidelines to take place.  As such I have no plans to revise the statutory guidelines at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has made of the impact of rising energy prices, driven by recent unrest in the Middle East, on school budgets.
answer_text: Financial pressures arising from increased energy costs on school budgets are kept under ongoing review by the Education Authority (EA), as funding authority for grant aided schools.My Department will continue to engage with the EA to seek updates on the impact of increased energy costs and will liaise closely with the Department of Finance to ensure it is fully sighted on any resulting financial pressure to the education budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of how collaboration between his Department and the Department of Health has (i) changed; or (ii) improved, since March 2024.
answer_text: Collaboration between the Departments is underpinned by the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 (the CSC Act 2015) which places a duty on all Northern Ireland government departments and children’s authorities to co-operate.While both Departments face competing priorities and pressures, there is regular and constructive collaboration in a range of areas at both a Ministerial level and at Official level. The Joint Health and Education Oversight Group (JHEOG), established during the COVID-19 pandemic, provides a structured forum for senior officials to collaborate and to identify areas where a strengthened partnership can improve the overall outcomes delivered by the Departments. The Group has recently been refreshed to focus on current priorities and is now meeting on a more frequent basis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any plans to launch public recruitment competitions for the Strategic Investment Board.
answer_text: A review of SIB was carried out in 2023 - 2024, with the report finalised in 2025.  The report recognised that SIB continues to be a very important organisation to the NICS and the wider public sector. It suggested that there is a need to consider its ongoing strategic role and made a number of recommendations in this respect.We are clear that SIB has a key role to play in providing strategic advice on capital investment, supporting delivery of our major projects and developing innovative approaches to support Executive priorities. This must be the focus of its work, and it will be important that incoming board members are aligned to that strategic role. We are currently considering proposals for a public appointment competition, taking account of the findings of the review and the future strategic role of SIB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration being given, as part of the current curriculum review, to ensuring that the history curriculum reflects the diversity of perspectives and experiences across our society.
answer_text: As part of the curriculum review, the Curriculum Taskforce is looking carefully at how the history curriculum can appropriately reflect the diversity of perspectives and experiences across modern society.The current curriculum is too vague, which means schools can make very different choices about what they teach. This often results in pupils receiving a narrow, uneven or limited understanding of the past.A revised curriculum will set out clearer expectations, ensuring that all pupils develop a broad and balanced range of historical knowledge across local, regional, European and global history. This includes studying a wide range of people, places and periods so that pupils can understand multiple viewpoints, see how events connect, and develop historical empathy, which lets them appreciate how people in the past understood their world, even when their experiences differ from our own.This approach will support a richer and more diverse understanding of the past.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the relaxation of procurement rules for schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is committed to working proactively with schools to ensure that procurement arrangements are as effective as possible, whilst also ensuring value for money and compliance with legislative requirements.  As part of this, the EA has been reviewing the end-to-end ordering and payment process to explore areas for driving efficiency and effectiveness that would benefit all stakeholders.  In this regard several work streams have already been progressed including: Introduction of a new Electronic Invoicing system designed to improve efficiencies in the processing and payment of supplier invoices; andExtension of the current Punchout solution which provides a streamlined ordering process via Punchout solution directly to suppliers’ online catalogues.  Expansion of Punchout is expected to have a direct benefit for the vast majority of schools with several key educational supplies contracts now moving across to this solution.  The impact of which will improve the buying experience for school users.These interventions are being progressed alongside the Schools Procurement Pilot Scheme, which is hoped will provide schools with greater autonomy in purchasing low-value consumables/orders, within their delegated budget.  The pilot will include alternative ordering and payment processes and allow schools the choice to use EA frameworks or use alternative suppliers within a defined threshold. EA engaged with the School Leadership Forum on 10 March 2026, to ensure that the Schools Procurement Pilot Scheme aligns with school leaders’ expectations and sought their views, including timing, governance arrangements and consideration of resourcing implications for schools.  A further focus group has been arranged for the 24 April 2026, the feedback from this process will enable the EA to review and refine its plan for implementation informed by school leaders’ feedback. It is expected that the pilot will be launched at the end of June 2026 and be live for a 6-month period, until 31 December 2026. These timelines will be subject to school leaders’ feedback.  During the implementation of the pilot the EA will provide support to schools through the provision of guidance materials and access to advisory support from EA colleagues. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the current curriculum review is considering how literacy provision will reflect the diverse range of authors, playwrights and writers from across the island of Ireland.
answer_text: The current curriculum does not prescribe specific authors, playwrights or writers. This leaves choices entirely to individual schools, resulting in wide disparities in what pupils study. In practice, this has led to many pupils receiving narrow, inconsistent and limited exposure to the rich breadth of literature available. There is no entitlement for all young people to encounter a balanced or diverse range of texts or genres.By contrast, the emerging proposals for a revised curriculum will ensure that all pupils engage with whole texts drawn from a broad span of carefully selected periods, cultures and traditions. This structured approach will give pupils meaningful and consistent exposure to a wider diversity of writers, rather than leaving such encounters to chance.Work is ongoing to finalise the revised framework ahead of full public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether (i) his Department has carried out any assessment of the challenges facing Irish Medium Education with regard to SEN provision; and (ii) his strategy for Irish Medium Education addresses these challenges.

These challenges were highlighted in a report produced by Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta in November 2024. Officials have undertaken initial engagement in this area to identify potential actions to develop SEN provision for IME. This will continue through the development of my Department’s IME Strategy, which will have a pillar focused on SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on nursing provision in special schools.
answer_text: I am committed to working with colleagues in Health and Education to ensure that all children with complex medical needs can be supported to access school and the curriculum through child specific care. Officials from my Department and Department of Health, along with representatives from the Education Authority and the Public Health Agency (PHA), have commenced work to develop a new model of care in special schools that best meets the healthcare needs of these children. This project is informed by the recommendations made in the PHA review of population health of children with complex medical needs attending special schools and it is intended that this work will determine the level of multi-agency resourcing required across professions, including nursing. Engagement with children, parents and families and staff working in special schools including teachers and classroom assistants and staff from the Health Trusts will be a key element of this work.

answer_text: (i) I am familiar with the campaign for Benedict’s Law. My officials have met with Helen Blythe, Founder of the Benedict Blythe Foundation, on a number of occasions, most recently in April 2025, to discuss her campaign to mandate school allergy policies, training and spare allergy pens in all educational settings.In Northern Ireland, educational settings have a responsibility to follow the “Supporting pupils with Medication Needs” guidance and make reasonable adjustments to ensure that the needs of pupils with medical needs, including severe allergies/anaphylaxis, are met. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment made of the cumulative impact of his Department’s proposed budget on arm’s-length bodies that support statutory and strategic objectives, including Irish-medium education; (ii) whether he is aware of any bodies facing potential staff redundancies as a result.
answer_text: The multi-year budget proposed by the Minister of Finance, which has not been agreed by the Executive and which remains out for public consultation until 3 March 2026, leaves my Department needing to make savings of c£0.9 billion in 2026-27, c£1.01bn in 2027-28 and c£1.15bn in 2028-29. My commitment is to minimise compulsory redundancies by prioritising redeployment and voluntary exit.  However, given that c82% of the education budget meets staffing costs, there is no reasonable prospect of my Department being able to live within the proposed budget in the short-term, without mass compulsory redundancies, which would require dedicated in-year funding of a similar level to the savings required.  Such redundancy funding does not currently exist.  Clearly, this would have a devastating impact on educational outcomes and undermine the very fabric of our system including my Department’s arm’s length bodies that support statutory and strategic objectives, including but not limited to Irish-medium education. In the absence of redundancies, I would be required to cut major schemes on which families and communities rely, including home-to-school transport, free school meals, school uniform grant, youth services and extended schools. The Northern Ireland Childcare Scheme would also become unsustainable.  Even after cuts of this magnitude my Department would still fall far short of the savings required.I trust the Member will make representations to his party colleague to avoid such an impact on the funding of Education in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether he was consulted on; or (ii) approved, changes introduced by the Education Authority to the Essential Car User Allowance Scheme and mileage rates for Education Authority staff; and (ii) any assessment made by his Department of the financial impact these changes may have on staff, and on the delivery of education services.
answer_text: The Department of Education was advised of this decision.The Education Authority has advised that during 2025, they undertook a review of their Travel and Subsistence Scheme. As part of this process, EA engaged in an extended period of negotiation with Trade Union Side on the proposed changes with negotiations beginning in April 2025 and concluding in December 2025 without agreement.  The travel scheme that was in place was both complex and unfair, with six different mileage rates applying to support staff, depending on their car engine size and whether they are deemed to be a casual or essential car user.  The changes that are being implemented will standardise the casual mileage rate for all staff, bringing support staff into line with teachers and the HMRC recommended rate. This same rate is also used by many other organisations across the wider public sector. The essential user rate has been proportionately amended in line with this change. The EA also operates an Essential Car User (ECU) Allowance scheme, which is remaining unchanged. However, due to improvements in the EA IT system, data is now available to ensure the eligibility criteria for this allowance are more consistently and accurately applied.  Staff must travel a certain distance each year to be eligible for the allowance and a review of the data found that some staff were not meeting the minimum mileage criteria.In the absence of an agreement with Trade Union Side, the EA decided to implement the new approach, in the interests of consistency, fairness and good use of public money.Given the severe and growing budgetary pressures across the education sector, the EA is actively seeking efficiencies across all its services and operations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why dyslexia, while recognised as a Special Educational Needs (SEN) category, is not listed as a medical category in his Department’s publication on SEN and Medical Categories Information for Parents and Young People; (ii) any consideration that has been given to including dyslexia as a listed medical category, given its prevalence; and (iii) whether the absence of dyslexia from the medical categories list has any implications for (a) the assessment of need; or (b) access to support, for children and young people with dyslexia.
answer_text: Dyslexia is a Specific Learning Difficulty related to the way in which information is learned and processed. It is identified and addressed through educational assessment and intervention rather than through medical diagnosis or treatment and therefore is not listed as a medical category. A full review of the SEN and Medical categories was undertaken in 2017/18 and also as part of the Department’s End to End Review of SEN. Both reviews confirmed Dyslexia as a SEN category.The absence of Dyslexia from the medical categories list does not have adverse implications for either:(a) the assessment of need, or(b) access to support.Pupils who are recorded on the SEN Register will have their individual learning needs addressed through targeted special educational provision, delivered as part of a structured and graduated approach to support. The EA’s Graduated Response Framework provides clear guidance to schools on how to support children with a range of specific needs such as Dyslexia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any barriers that delay the cross-border registration of qualified teachers who wish to teach locally.
answer_text: The Department is unaware of any barriers which would prevent teachers educated in the Republic of Ireland from securing registration with the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) and working in our schools.Between 2020 and 2025, GTCNI received 69 applications from teachers educated in the Republic of Ireland while processing over 7,100 total registrations. Of the 69 applications, 56 (81.2%) were accepted onto GTCNI’s register.  Of the 13 applications refused, four applicants held no initial teaching education (ITE) qualification, four did not meet our requirement for teachers to hold qualifications equivalent to GCSE grade C passes in Mathematics and/or Science; and five had completed ITE qualifications which had clear deficits when compared to Northern Ireland delivered Degree or Postgraduate Certificate of Education courses.  GTCNI carries a responsibility to protect the quality of the teaching workforce in Northern Ireland; and these refusals were all correct decisions, taken in line with GTCNI’s qualification rules.It is notable that since January 2024 the average registration processing times for applicants holding an ITE qualification obtained in the Republic of Ireland has been just 3.7 weeks, well inside GTCNI’s stated 8 weeks processing target for any applicant holding a non-United Kingdom issued qualification. This performance is also directly comparable to the current average processing time for applicants holding a Northern Ireland ITE qualification, confirming that teachers coming from the Republic of Ireland are not being disadvantaged in any way.GTCNI’s information management system shows that in almost every case where an applicant’s registration was delayed beyond its target processing time, this was caused an initial failure by the applicant to supply all of the information required for registration; or to a delay by an applicant in supplying missing information once the omission was brought to the applicant’s attention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work underway to review existing anti-bullying legislation and policies, with a view to ensuring they remain up to date and effective.
answer_text: While no formal review or evaluation of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (The Act) and associated policies has undertaken by the Department, my officials monitor the implementation and impact of the Act on an ongoing basis with a view to informing related policy and practice.Recognising that access to comprehensive data is key to informing post-legislative scrutiny of the Act and effective, evidence-based policy-making, over the last 18 months the Department has been able to take forward a number of measures to improve the bullying-related data the Department has access to. These measures include the introduction of bullying-related questions in the Northern Ireland School Omnibus Survey, engaging with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to review how schools are implementing the Act, and receiving regular Outcome Based Accountability reports from the Education Authority’s (EA) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT).  The ETI is also undertaking further visits to primary schools to review how strategies for addressing bullying, and the Department is working with the EA with the aim of digitising the Bullying Concern Assessment Form through the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) Programme with a view to facilitating access to comprehensive anonymised regional data on schools’ bullying related data.While no formal review of the legislation and policies has been undertaken, the emerging evidence from the available data sources demonstrates high levels of compliance with the Act and strong evidence of resulting positive impacts.  The Act has undoubtedly helped to provide greater consistency in how all schools respond to bullying incidents and allegations, helping to build on schools’ duty of care for their pupils, and strengthening the protection pupils can expect if they experience bullying type behaviour in school by ensuring all pupils are protected to the same, best practice standards.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline how urgent maintenance and repair works in schools are prioritised.
answer_text: Due to budget constraints, maintenance and repair works in schools are currently prioritised on the basis of statutory compliance and the need to address urgent and emergency issues which could close a school or present a significant health and safety risk.The Education Authority’s Maintenance Service triage requests received through the Maintenance Helpdesk, with the response categorised according to risk. Response maintenance is undertaken by the Education Authority’s Term Service Short Contractors.  For larger scale projects, the works may be transferred to the Minor Works programme. The severe and unsustainable financial pressures have meant there is insufficient funding to carry out any planned maintenance or invest in long-term improvements. This reactive approach cannot continue and the temporary closure of Nazareth House, as well as the closure of a building at St Eugene’s Primary School only serves to highlight the need for increased and sustained investment to allow for a programme of planned, preventative maintenance. I remain committed to improving the condition of our school estate and have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment.The Executive needs to provide this sustained and increased investment in the education estate. The current situation cannot continue.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools that have signed up to use the InnerDrive online learning platform; and (ii) the number of individual teachers who have (a) registered; or (b) completed courses.
answer_text: An approved business case is in place for the programme. This level of expenditure is within my Department’s delegated limits and does not require Department of Finance approval.All requirements for Direct Award Contracts were followed, including Accounting Office approval. The commitment in the TransformED Delivery Plan required the roll out of an online Science of Learning programme for post-primary teachers. This was in response to the need to ensure our teachers are equipped with the techniques to support memory retention, attention, metacognition and effective learning strategies, an urgent need also highlighted in the strategic review of the curriculum. This is a highly specialised area of professional development and one which the Education Authority did not possess the expertise and experience to deliver. An external provider was therefore required. Due to the bespoke nature of the product and to ensure it was available for the start of the 2025/26 academic year, InnerDrive was appointed under a Direct Award Contract. A significant bulk purchase discount was secured for the product to ensure Value for Money. A full evaluation of the programme is being conducted by the Education and Training Inspectorate and a Post Project Evaluation will also be conducted. A total of 161 schools have signed up for InnerDrive’s Teacher CPD Academy and as a condition of participation schools were required to incorporate the professional learning into whole-school planning, utilise one school development day to reflect on the programme, ensure attendance at all live webinars and participate in an evaluation of the programme. As of 11 November 2025, 9,858 teachers have registered and have spent 19,205 hours on the programme. 4,165 online professional learning courses completed, some as part of whole-school training and completed by multiple teachers but included only once in the figure above. These courses are also supported by a range of resources including Teaching Unpacked, Playlists, Student Lessons and the Cognitive Science Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34275/22-27, to detail (i) whether he met with his officials in charge of the Uniforms bill on three occasions; and (ii) whether he will deposit in the Assembly Library the minutes of all meetings undertaken by officials, within his Department, in relation to the Uniforms Bill.
answer_text: I can confirm that (i) yes, I met my officials on the three occasions evidenced by the relevant minutes deposited in the Assembly Library.  In relation to your request at (ii) I will not deposit minutes of all meetings undertaken by my officials.  There were many internal discussions between officials, representing the free and frank exchange of views for the purposes of deliberation at various stages of development of the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill.  Meetings between my officials and the Office of the Legislative Counsel and Departmental Solicitors Office related to the provision of legal advice which, you will appreciate, is subject to legal privilege.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail if consideration has been given to extending his plans for bursaries, and other financial support, to trainee teachers who have already begun their studies.
answer_text: My Department recognises the dedication of students who have commenced Initial Teacher Education (ITE), including the financial commitment required. Their decision to pursue a career in teaching is deeply valued and appreciated.As announced, the bursary scheme will apply to new entrants of relevant subjects, beginning their ITE programmes from September 2026 onwards. The purpose of the bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages.   Extending eligibility to current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the pilot. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.  I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail if his announcement on bursaries and financial support for certain trainee teachers will apply to those studying to become primary school teachers in the Irish-medium Education sector.
answer_text: The purpose of the bursary scheme is to increase the number of applications and enrolments in Initial Teacher Education (ITE) in those post-primary subjects experiencing critical teacher shortages. These include Mathematics, Chemistry, Physics, Computing, Technology & Design and the IM Post-Primary Enhancement Programme. The bursary scheme does not therefore extend to ITE at primary level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how the Initial Teacher Education Bursary Scheme will be aligned with wider workforce planning for Irish-medium education, including the recruitment and retention of appropriately qualified special educational needs support staff.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of aligning the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) Bursary Scheme with broader workforce planning objectives, including those specific to Irish-medium Education and the provision of special educational needs (SEN) support.To this end, my officials continue to extensively engage with delivery partners and Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta to support the further development of post-primary ITE provision as a key component of a sustainable and skilled teaching workforce. This includes both the current PGCE provision at post-primary and my Department is also engaging with stakeholders to facilitate the development of further Irish-medium post-primary options, to further supplement the sector. The ITE Bursary Scheme will support high-calibre students to pursue teaching careers where recruitment challenges are most acute. This includes places for the Irish-medium Post-Primary Enhancement Programme and where demand exceeds supply, the Department will consider the specific needs of the sector when finalising ITE intakes. In recognition of the importance of ensuring that schools are adequately staffed to deliver high-quality education for children and young people with SEN, the Education Authority recently launched a pilot recruitment scheme for classroom assistants. This scheme, currently operating in selected areas, aims to streamline recruitment processes and improve access to suitably qualified support staff by enabling schools to draw from a ready-to-deploy pool of candidates, significantly reducing recruitment timeframes.

answer_text: At no stage was my view sought, or permission given, to approve officials making this commitment to the committee. For reasons I have outlined in the Assembly, guidelines follow the law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) the staffing composition of a Local Impact Team; (ii) the training or qualification required to work as part of a Local Impact Team; and (iii) the terms and conditions of employment for workers employed as part of a Local Impact Team.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;All Local IMPACT Teams (LiTs) include a cohort of Teacher grade specialist staff, but some also include Intervention Officers, Behavioural Support Assistants and/or Classroom Assistants.  Each LiT is managed by a Senior Teacher or Assistant Advisory Officer (AAO), and all LiTs within each Health Trust Area are overseen by an Area Manager at Advisor or Education Officer grade.As the LiT staff cohort has transitioned over from eight pre-cursor Pupil Support Services, the minimum qualification and experience requirements vary according to the grade of staff and the pre-cursor service. However the majority of frontline LiT staff are at Teacher or Advisory Teacher grade, all of whom have to be fully qualified teachers with sufficient experience and/or additional qualifications relating to a particular SEN category to allow them to have met the requirements of the original recruiting service.The terms and conditions of employment for LiT staff are explicitly designed to fully respect the existing terms and conditions of all staff, as dictated by their original contract which varies by staffing grade, pre-cursor service and in some cases, Education and Library Board service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the independent panel which was established to review Baseline School Funding.
answer_text: On 3 July I announced the appointment of an independent panel to undertake the Review of Baseline School Funding Provided Through the Common Funding Formula.As outlined in paragraph 17 of the Terms of Reference, the panel is actively engaging with a broad range of key stakeholders across the education sector to inform the Review. The Panel has been asked to conclude its work by Halloween 2025 and provide a report to me shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department is undertaking to reduce barriers to cross-border mobility for teachers.
answer_text: The Department is unaware of any significant barriers restricting teacher mobility between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.The General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) and The Teaching Council in the Republic of Ireland (TCI) have well-established mechanisms to assess and register teachers who have undertaken their initial teacher education in the other jurisdiction.  GTCNI has confirmed that TCI registered teachers generally secure registration at their first attempt and within its normal application processing times.The North South Ministerial Council met in its Education Sectoral format on 23 May 2024 and 2 April 2025.  No teacher mobility concerns were raised at either meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will engage with the General Teaching Council and instruct them to remove the barrier of the requirement for newly-qualified teachers in the Republic of Ireland to complete one years' work experience in the north before being able to work here.
answer_text: The General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) has advised the Department that it has no requirement for a newly-qualified teacher whose Initial Teacher Education (ITE) qualification was obtained in the Republic of Ireland to complete one year of work experience in Northern Ireland before being able to secure GTCNI registration.Without GTCNI registration no-one, irrespective of where they have completed their ITE, can legally teach in a grant aided school in Northern Ireland; completely precluding any possibility of them obtaining further work experience here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the General Teaching Council requirement that newly-qualified teachers in the Republic of Ireland must complete one years' work experience before being able to work here, and whether this complies with the Memorandum of Understanding that was signed between the British and Irish Government in 2019 to continue with the mutual recognition of qualifications across the island and for regulatory bodies to comply with this.
answer_text: The General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) has advised the Department that it has no requirement for a newly-qualified teacher whose Initial Teacher Education (ITE) qualification was obtained in the Republic of Ireland to complete one year of work experience in Northern Ireland before being able to secure GTCNI registration.Without GTCNI registration no-one, irrespective of where they have completed their ITE, can legally teach in a grant aided school in Northern Ireland; completely precluding any possibility of them obtaining further work experience here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is aware that, due to General Teaching Council requirements, newly-qualified teachers in the Republic of Ireland must complete one years' work experience in the north before being able to work here.
answer_text: The General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) has advised the Department that it has no requirement for a newly-qualified teacher whose Initial Teacher Education (ITE) qualification was obtained in the Republic of Ireland to complete one year of work experience in Northern Ireland before being able to secure GTCNI registration.Without GTCNI registration no-one, irrespective of where they have completed their ITE, can legally teach in a grant aided school in Northern Ireland; completely precluding any possibility of them obtaining further work experience here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Employment Support Scheme.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any issues he is aware of pertaining to the awarding of back pay to teachers following recent pay awards; and (ii) if there is a specific issue when it comes to awarding back pay to teachers that have taken maternity leave.
answer_text: Agreement on the Teachers Pay Award with effect from 1 September 2024 was reached on 4 April 2025, and the Department and teacher employers agreed to pay the arrears due to all teachers as soon as was practically possibly after the date of agreement.The Teachers’ Pay Team worked diligently to apply the terms of the agreement and I can confirm that new pay rates and arrears payments were paid to 4,406 supply teachers on 19 May 2025 and to 17,934 permanent teachers on 30 May 2025 including a total of 188 teachers who had left teaching service since 1 September 2024.In regard to any issues pertaining to the awarding of back pay to teachers;To date there have been no issues raised pertaining to the award of back pay to teachers in general.  Queries relating to arrears payments have been minimal.To date there have been no specific issues raised which indicate there were errors in the award of back pay to teachers that have taken maternity leave.  Arrears payments of maternity pay for teachers are not included in the main figure for arrears of salary as they are required to be paid as Statutory or Occupational Maternity pay. In the small number of cases where queries have arisen regarding payment of arrears for maternity Teachers Pay Team has been able to confirm that the payments have been processed correctly.  Please note;Answer refers to teachers’ salaries processed through the Teachers Pay Team’s payroll system and does not include Voluntary Grammar schools.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Communities in Transition programme.
answer_text: The Communities in Transition programme has delivered £24 million of support across eight designated areas since 2019. This year CIT continues to operate across the same designated areas, 35 contracts have been awarded from a total budget of £2.9 million.Building on the learning to date, this year CIT will focus on hard-hitting issues such as Mental Health and Addictions, Raising the Aspirations of Young People, addressing exploitation, and addressing the Impact of Paramilitarism on the Physical Landscape.  Restorative Practice and ex-prisoner support will continue on a regional basis.

To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on dormant asset funding.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the high number of traffic collisions near the Mount Alverno petrol station on the Springfield Road, Belfast, to outline when traffic lights or alternative safe crossing, will be introduced at this location to address the ongoing safety concerns.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many sites that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how many companies have become accredited as Living Wage employers.
answer_text: The Real Living Wage franchise in the north was successfully launched last year by Advice NI, and my department continues to provide support as part of a three-year funding commitment.		Increasing the number of Real Living Wage employers will contribute towards achieving my economic vision, and it will help to drive up the number of people here in Good Jobs.		159 employers have now been accredited as Real Living Wage organisations, and between them, these organisations employ nearly 5000 people. 		I met with the Real Living Wage franchise and Advice NI this morning and we discussed the positive progress that has been made since its launch.  We discussed how we can continue working together to promote Good Jobs and achieve better opportunities for all.In April of this year the Real Living Wage rate increased to £12.60 per hour.  This illustrates the importance of our continued focus on Good Jobs. My Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, the Real Living Wage franchise, and the development of a Good Jobs charter are all critical and exciting strands for improving working lives and delivering good jobs for workers in the north.  Employers will also see real benefits.  Good jobs increase productivity.  They are good for business, and they increase employee retention and commitment.  Good jobs attract investment and create a stronger economy that is fair for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail: (i) how many children in the Belfast area have a Statement of Special Educational Needs; and (ii) how many of these children are receiving their full allocation of hours as per their Statement.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:based on the most recent information held there are 6971 children/young people with statements in schools with postcodes within the Belfast Local Government District area.this information is not held by the EA – employment and management is delegated to schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the criteria for residents' parking schemes.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount spent on the TransformED School Leaders’ conference in Belfast, which took place on the 8th of May.
answer_text: The cost of the TransformED Conference was £44,100.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any schools that have been given assurances that any of their classroom or resource space that has been utilised for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools will be replaced; and (ii) whether funding for the replacement of these spaces will come from the special educational needs budget or the minor works budget.
answer_text: Where possible, new specialist provision in mainstream classes are being established in schools utilising existing surplus space.  To meet the demand for specialist provision places for September 2025 the Education Authority’s focus is currently being directed to delivering the accommodation for the new specialist provision classes, with temporary rental accommodation being provided for any displaced mainstream classes; permanent replacement accommodation works will be brought forward for delivery after September 2025, subject to budget availability. Funding for works will be met from the special educational needs capital investment programme budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) issues; and (ii) areas of work the forthcoming Irish-medium Education Strategy will seek to address.
answer_text: Key areas of work have been identified by stakeholders in the Irish-medium education sector that will be taken forward in the development of an Irish-medium Education Strategy and Action Plan.These key areas are:1. 	Resourcing2.	Educator Recruitment and Training3.	Curriculum and Assessment Development4.	Sustainability and Growth of Schools5.	Parental and Community Support6.	Policy and Strategic Development7.	Supporting Students with Special EducationalNeeds / Disabilities (SEND)This list is not exhaustive but will provide a framework for the continuing development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a timeline for the development and publication of his Department's Irish-medium Education Strategy.
answer_text: I have recently approved the development of a Department-led Irish-medium Education Strategy and Action Plan. The proposed timescale for development and publication of this would be mid 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how he intends to address teaching staff’s concerns regarding workload.
answer_text: Nine previously agreed Workforce Reviews included recommendations on workload and a range of these have already been implemented. It was announced on Friday 28 March that following individual consultation with members, the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) was not in a position to ratify an agreement on Management Side’s revised formal offer to resolve the teachers’ Pay dispute 2024/25 and the second pay offer, which included commitments around workload, was rejected.I am pleased to confirm that constructive negotiations took place with trade unions in recent days where I listened to their concerns about workload. I therefore proposed a commission to conduct an Independent Review of all aspects of workload, that will be taken forward by a three-member panel, comprising an independent chair, a member nominated by the unions and a member nominated by Management Side. The Panel will examine workload and report to me by the end of November 2025. It will also monitor the implementation of the suite of workload commitments issued alongside the Management Side pay offer.I recently launched a new education strategy, TransformED, which will reform and improve education delivery in Northern Ireland. It contains a range of commitments that are designed to drive improvement but also reduce workload.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action he will take to address the inequity faced by Irish-medium schools which are categorised under Other Maintained and do not have access to the likes of human resource services and legal services.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges that face Irish-medium schools that are categorised as Other Maintained.I have recently approved the development of an Irish-medium Strategy. Officials are in the early stages of developing a Strategy and Action Plan, which will aim to address many of the long-standing challenges that face the IM sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated by his Department and the Education Authority to support bespoke capacity building for educators within Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: The Department of Education has provided funding under ‘A Fair Start’ to Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) to progress Key Actions 6(ii) and 6(iii) of that report. Key Action 6(ii) states “DE should provide additional focused support for the Irish Medium sector in the form of educational resources, Initial Teacher Education, TPL and leadership training”. CnaG received funding of £100k to contribute to this in 2024/25.The Education Authority allocates £37k of its core budget to a Learning Leaders Support Scheme which has been used for individual bursaries to build capacity to support the advancement of Irish-medium education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated by his Department (i) to support the Irish Medium Leadership Group; and (ii) for the development of Irish Medium Learning Communities.
answer_text: Previously the Department of Education provided £14k per year to the Education Authority (EA) to support an Irish-medium Leadership Working Group. Unfortunately, due to a challenging budget position, this earmarked funding was removed in 2023/24.There is currently no dedicated funding provided by the Department of Education for the development of Irish-medium Learning Communities.These funding streams will be reviewed as part of the development of an Irish-medium Strategy and Action Plan, which is currently being taken forward by officials in my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to make any additional resources available to support key areas within Irish-medium schools such as special educational needs support, workforce, specialist subject teachers and accommodation.
answer_text: I am aware of the unique challenges faced by Irish-medium schools in Northern Ireland.As such, I have approved the development of an Irish-medium Education Strategy by the Department of Education. Officials are in the early stages of developing a Strategy and Action Plan, which will aim to address many of the long-standing challenges that face the Irish-medium sector.This strategy will take into consideration the issues outlined, and recommendations will be made based on this development. I cannot comment on the resource requirements until this process is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will engage with CCEA on the need to translate their key literacy and numeracy resources in order to better support pupils and educators in Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: I am aware that curriculum and assessment development, including resources, are an issue across the Irish-medium sector.I have recently approved the development of an Irish-medium Strategy. Officials are in the early stages of developing a Strategy and Action Plan, which will aim to address many of the long-standing challenges that face the IM sector.As a key stakeholder, officials will engage with CCEA in the development of the Irish-medium Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current (i) joint initiatives; and (ii) joined-up working in place between his Department and the Department of Education.
answer_text: The following are the areas in which my Department is working with the Department of Education.DE is represented on the Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for Carers which was established by the Social Care Collaborative Forum. Health and Education collaborate, through the Safeguarding Board for NI on Adverse Childhood Experiences and Trauma Informed Practice. Both Departments work with DoJ in response to the Gillen Review of Serious Sexual Offences.DoH administers the Surestart programme on behalf of DE.DE participates in the DoH-led Reform Programme for Children’s Social Care Services.DE has initiated the process of establishing an ‘expert writing group’, bringing in professional expertise from early years and health professionals to support policy development work and develop in detail a suite of fully formed and costed programmes in preparation for potential SEN Transformation funding and / or further Early Learning and Childcare Strategy (ELC) funding.   DE is currently represented on the NI Bereavement Network (NIBN) which was established by DoH in April 2021 in response to one of the recommendations in the ‘COVID-19 Guidance: Bereavement Advice and Support’ report.Officials in DoH sit on a cross departmental working group with colleagues from DE and DfC in relation to gambling.  MCA Unit within DoH has been engaging with DE officials in relation to the implementation of the Mental Capacity Act in education settings.DoH officials are represented on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy Programme Board and Task and Finish Group.DE are included in a multi-agency group working on the development of Early Help and a Multiagency Front Door.DE contribute to the Protect Life 2 Strategy.DoH Property Management Branch is working with DE on property related matters in terms of re-use of DoH land which has been declared surplus to health needs and liaison on dealing with Backlog Maintenance Liability.DoH policy officials representing Mental Health and Learning Disability have been engaging with DE counterparts to share learning from the development and implementation of the Regional Policy on the use of Restrictive Practices in Health & Social Care settings, with a view to informing DE's development of statutory guidance in educational settings.  Those discussions are ongoing.As part of the Sexual Health Action Plan, there is an action to work with DE to consider opportunities for the further development of relationships and sexuality education. Health Improvement Policy Branch links in with DE to share information and actively assess whether opportunities exist for collaboration in the interest of improving sexual health and wellbeing outcomes education.DoH is in discussions about becoming involved in DE's Ending Violence Against Women and Girls work on Healthy Relationships Forum.The Chief Nursing Officer (CNO) commissioned the Public Health Agency (PHA) to work in partnership with the Education Authority (EA) to undertake a comprehen

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink will assess the frequency of the city bound bus service from Assumption Grammar School to ensure (i) there is enough supply for demand; and (ii) buses arrive on time, as students are often have to wait on the next bus due to full capacity.
answer_text: The provision of dedicated school transport services is a matter for the Education Authority.  Regular scheduled services are an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink operates two dedicated school buses from Assumption Grammar to Belfast, service Q18 at 1525 and 1545. In addition, there are scheduled stage carriage services on the 520 and 518a at 1511, 1600 and 1657. Translink are not aware of any capacity issues. Any additional provision of service would need additional funding.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: The applications for (i) Minor Works; and (ii) School Enhancement Projects that have been rejected since 2019 are set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.
