answer_text: A copy of the meeting note will be deposited in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the financial support in place for the Buddy Bear School in Dungannon, given its approval as suitable for the admission of children with special educational needs.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to AQW 17962/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 29 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on waiting times for adults who are awaiting assessment for an autism diagnosis; and (ii) to detail any work being undertaken in relation to this.
answer_text: (i)Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of Autism Services. HSCTS have outlined the following approximate current waiting times for adults awaiting assessment for an Autism diagnosis.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT): 312 weeks.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT): 86 weeksBelfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT): 188 weeksWestern Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT): 173 weeks.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT): 240 weeks(ii)SHSCT advise the Adult Autism Diagnostic Service has increased assessment clinics from once per month to weekly in an effort to address demand and capacity issues.NHSCT note an ongoing focus on recruitment to ensure posts are filled and that any temporary loss in capacity within the service (e.g. maternity leave) is covered where possible.  The NHSCT Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder Service is progressing the option of using waiting list initiative funding to outsource diagnostic assessments to independent sector providers for those experiencing the longest wait to access the service.  The Trust intends to use allocated funding to outsource approximately 120 diagnostic assessments.  The service is currently engaging with independent sector providers, alongside colleagues within Waiting List Services, to operationalise this project with the aim of assessments being completed in early 2025.BHSCT advise waiting lists have arisen from demand being greater than funded capacity and challenges in maintaining staffing at full capacity in a specialist service (impacted by challenges in recruitment and difficulties backfilling posts e.g. maternity leave). The Trust further note newly appointed staff require training before being able to work at full capacity given the specialist nature of the posts. In order to support increased capacity when there are vacancies, the Trust has in place a contract with an independent sector provider.WHSCT advise the numbers being referred for diagnostic assessment have increased yearly over the past five years. The service has capacity to complete approximately 60 new assessments per year (+/- 10); depending on complexity. Over the past number of years, when financially possible, the WHSCT have employed extra staff contracted into the Trust to assist with diagnostic assessments and have considered waiting list initiatives in an attempt to use all available resources to make use of extra clinics; including weekend clinics to assist in the reduction of waiting times. SEHSCT advise various approaches have been implemented to address capacity issues and growing demand including updating the short-term intervention service model to a predominately group-based intervention model, to enable capacity to be redirected to completing diagnostic assessments. SEHSCT also cite funding an external provider to deliver a group work programme and redirect additional capacity to complete diagnostic assessments. SEHSCT have rolled out training sess

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has assessed the impact of increases in the cost of living on student nurses in receipt of a bursary.
answer_text: I am very much aware of the adverse impact of the cost of living crisis on students on pre-registration nursing programmes commissioned by my Department. However, any action to address this issue will have to be affordable. In 27 September 2022, in response to the cost of living crisis, my predecessor as Minister, Robin Swann, announced changes to the rates of specific means-tested bursary allowances available to pre-registration nursing and midwifery students. This included increases of 15% in the dependants’ allowance for spouse or first child; the allowance for subsequent children; and parents’ learning allowance. The mileage allowance for students on clinical placement was also increased to bring it into line with current NICS public transport rates.I am currently exploring with my officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on all pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department, including nursing and midwifery.This work will help to identify where the available support should best be targeted.  My officials will be liaising with the universities and student representatives in order to understand better the issues involved. I anticipate that they will wish to consider evidence such as the levels of attrition out of pre-registration training programmes and the numbers of nursing and midwifery students accessing university hardship funds.Any proposed changes will be subject to wider consultation with a range of key stakeholders. Proposals will need to take into account affordability, in the context of the well-documented financial pressures facing my Department and the wider Health and Social Care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the company that will provide the phone pouches for use under his Department's pilot to limit the use of mobile phones in schools; and (ii) the processes involved in selecting the delivery partner of these pouches.
answer_text: No company has yet been identified as the supplier of phone pouches for the Department’s pilot to test the effectiveness of using mobile phone inhibiting devices in schools in Northern Ireland.Officials are currently working through the established business case process and are engaging with the Education Authority Commercial Procurement Service with a view to initiating the procurement process through which a supplier will be appointed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the Libraries for Primaries campaign findings that 41 per cent of primary schools currently lack access to a dedicated library space, to detail the steps he plans to take to address this gap and increase the number of primary schools with dedicated library facilities.
answer_text: I acknowledge the importance of pupils having access to a range of books to enable them to discover and develop the joy of reading. Regretably, there is currently insufficient capital funding to significantly increase the number of primary schools with dedicated library facilities. In new build primary schools of more than seven classes a separate library is provided. While for new build schools with less than seven classes, the library facility is co-located within a resource area. Existing schools can create class libraries within classrooms or resource spaces and pupils can also access a range of books and other reading and audio materials through their local, mobile or online library.  The extremely constrained capital budget has meant that difficult decisions continue to be required and available investment has had to be prioritised to keep schools open, pupils safe and provide education access for the most vulnerable. I will, however, continue to make the case for increased and sustained funding for the schools’ estate so that every child can be educated in a school which fully supports their learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As required by the legislation, the Department published the statutory report (the Section 10 Report) on Integrated Education on 26 April 2024. Officials are continuing to work closely with the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) and the Education Authority (EA) to implement relevant actions within the S10 report.In addition, a draft revised Strategy has now been shared with NICIE, CSSC and IEF as part of the consultation requirements under Section 3 of the Act. The revised Strategy and Action Plan will be updated and opened to public consultation following due consideration of their comments before final publication in spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures his Department is taking to undertake its statutory duty to encourage, facilitate and support integrated education.
answer_text: In delivering this duty my Department seeks to respond positively to parental demand for Integrated provision and works to meet the needs of the sector in a range of areas, for example in considering home to school transport requirements and school requests for temporary variations to their admissions and/or enrolment numbers.  Additionally, a range of bespoke, sector specific investment is provided to support the development of Integrated Education.  This includes annual funding support and ongoing business planning support to the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education, support to transforming schools and significant capital investment via the Fresh Start Agreement. The Department has also adapted and flexibly applied policies and processes to support the ongoing growth of the sector in line with parental demand.Representatives and advocates of Integrated Education are included in departmental working and strategic groups providing a vehicle for dialogue, input and for their views to be considered. This engagement will continue to be an integral part of the Department’s approach and the Integrated sector will continue to be an important voice alongside others in shaping policy design.The Education Authority has confirmed that all Controlled Integrated schools have access to all EA services and Grant Maintained Integrated schools have access to a significant range of EA services.My Department also requires its Non-Departmental Public Bodies to support the delivery of this statutory duty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools currently operating breakfast clubs; and (ii) whether any consideration is being given to increase the funding for this.
answer_text: Information on breakfast clubs is not routinely collected from schools. The Department’s Extended Schools (ES) programme provides additional funding for those schools serving the most disadvantaged areas to provide for a wide range of services and activities, which can include breakfast clubs. According to the latest Extended Schools annual report (2022/23), 183 schools used ES funding to provide a breakfast club for students. I have safeguarded the funding for the Extended Schools programme this year and have asked my officials to commission an evaluation of the Extended Schools programme. I have no immediate plans to increase the funding for the Extended Schools programme or for breakfast clubs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for the criteria for RAISE programme funding that will see some grammar schools in affluent areas benefit from funding, but other schools in areas of high derivation will not receive funding.
answer_text: No funding has been allocated under the RAISE programme as yet.   The intent of the programme is to support children, young people and communities in areas of deprivation and disadvantage – with a key design feature being that it is based on where children live, not where they go to school. Another design feature is that it will operate across Northern Ireland, with a presence in each Local Government District area.RAISE localities were selected using objective criteria based on a range of data sets. These were analysed to bring forward a comprehensive set of (child focused) indicators which incorporate educational disadvantage factors and those relating to socio-economic disadvantage including poverty, health and crime/anti-social behaviour.  RAISE localities were then ranked in descending GCSE achievement order from lowest to highest.The schools that serve RAISE localities have been identified as a potential key stakeholder, along with children and young people, families and communities, the voluntary and community sector, councils and other public sector organisations.  This is not a guarantee of funding for those schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he discussed education policies, including plans to build an Irish Language school in East Belfast, with the Loyalist Communities Council at their meeting on 24 September 2024.
answer_text: Mr David Campbell, Chairman of the Loyalist Communities Council, wrote to me in March 2024 requesting a meeting to discuss educational underachievement in loyalist communities.  I accepted this invitation, and a meeting was scheduled for July 2024, which was then rearranged for 24 September.  The focus of the meeting was to discuss approaches and programmes to tackle educational underachievement and disadvantage.  The meeting lasted around 50 minutes.  Before the meeting concluded, the LCC representatives raised concerns about what they perceived as being a lack of community support for an Irish Medium school in East Belfast.The only action point from the meeting was for officials to share information on the RAISE programme which is intended to take a locality-based approach to raise aspiration and tackle underachievement in areas of disadvantage.  I have placed a copy of the minutes of this meeting in the Assembly library for the attention of members.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any additional resources her Department can provide to address the increased levels of antisocial behaviour at the interface between Springfield Road and Springmartin Estate; and (ii) any other initiatives she will consider to improve community cohesion in these areas.
answer_text: Policing and Community Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board play a key role in the Departments operational response to community safety concerns at a local level. Tackling antisocial behaviour (ASB) is a key strategic priority for PCSPs, which engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful, structured interventions to address the issue in each Council area.West Belfast DPCSP funds the Community Safety Initiative (CSI) and the Network Support Project (NSP) which work with local stakeholders and PCSP Members to improve policing and community safety in West Belfast. These projects have been dealing with ASB at Springfield Road / Springmartin Estate. NSP and CSI have also been liaising with youth providers Corpus Christie on street, supporting the new Black Mountain Shared Space and have engaged with some of the parents whose children have been involved in ASB. Staff have also circulated advisory messages on the risks and dangers of this type of behaviour and have asked parents to be mindful of where their children are. There has been engagement with representatives in all of the community who have acted similarly.West Belfast DPCSP also fund the Detached Youth Engagement project and there have been deployments of late specifically to focus on the Springfield/Springmartin interface which will remain under review. In addition, as the area falls between both North and West Belfast, Blackmountain Action Group was recently added to the North Belfast detached youth engagement partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to provide further detail on his recent announcement of the establishment of a delivery and implementation group to lead in the development of a management authority to support the controlled schools’ sector; and (ii) to detail the envisioned roles, functions and remit of the management authority upon its development.
answer_text: The recent Independent Review of Education highlighted that “The managing authority role has always been a challenge because the EA also provides a wide range of services to all other schools. This results in complicated systems for school management, which are, in particular, suboptimal for the Controlled sector. This has been compounded by the terms of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which imposes on the EA the duty to support integrated education”.In my recent statement to the Assembly, I reflected that the current system was failing controlled schools, due to its construct, as per the findings of the Independent Review.This is why I have commissioned a Taskforce to develop a proposed model for controlled schools support, including a dedicated Body, to include managing authority responsibility. For the shorter-term, it will also identify key performance issues and required improvements for controlled schools within the current model. The Taskforce has been asked to report back to the me by December 2024. The report should provide a proposed model, implementation roadmap outlining the legislative and practical steps required to establish a new model, and a timetable for my consideration. At that point I hope to be in a position to discuss roles, functions and remit of the managing authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the criteria used to determine schools' eligibility for funding as part of the RAISE programme; (ii) how the criteria was decided upon; and (iii) for an update in relation to the roll-out of the RAISE programme.
answer_text: RAISE is a Northern Ireland wide programme which has a presence in each Local Government District.  The selection criteria for geographical RAISE localities were based on a wide range of relevant underachievement and disadvantage data with GCSE attainment determining educational underachievement.  To aid the first stage of co-design, over 400 schools were identified that have ten or more pupils living in RAISE localities.  This is not a guarantee of funding for those schools identified and, likewise, having fewer than ten pupils in the localities does not mean a school cannot be involved in the programme.  My Department is open to including more areas and more schools as part of the detailed engagement process to follow and as we continue to develop the programme in liaison with all relevant stakeholders.  The methodology used for the selection of RAISE localities reflects findings and recommendations from the work of the Expert Panel which published its report ‘A Fair Start’ in June 2021.  It also reflects discussions which took place with the Stakeholder Reference Group between October 2021 and September 2023.  It is intended to consider issues relating to addressing disadvantage and learners at risk of exclusion from education as noted in the Independent Review of Education Report published in 2023. Development of the RAISE programme is progressing well and a number of key milestones have been achieved.  A Memorandum of Understanding was signed with the Department of Education in the Republic of Ireland in August this year.  Engagement began with schools in June 2024 and has continued with political representatives, council representatives, government departments and voluntary and community sector organisations.  Community Engagement Officers were appointed in September 2024 to lead on building effective collaborations in each locality, with engagement at a local level involving all relevant stakeholders planned for this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has in place to improve employment rights for supply teachers.
answer_text: Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the 5 main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC, and with limited resources, my officials are working with the employers of teachers to improve employment rights for supply teachers.A review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out Through the TNC and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any consideration that has been given to the fact that backdated payments from the Education Authority pay settlement will impact some workers' Universal Credit entitlements; and (ii) how he will address this issue.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 15202/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 27th September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the roll-out of the RAISE programme.
answer_text: Development of the RAISE programme is progressing well and a number of key milestones have been achieved. Engagement with schools began in June 2024 and engagement during the summer has included political representatives, council representatives, government departments and voluntary and community sector organisations. Eight Community Engagement Officers have been appointed and they will lead on building effective collaborations in each locality.  The team will be led by a Programme Manager for Communications and Engagement.  A Memorandum of Understanding was signed with the Department of Education in the Republic of Ireland on 30 August 2024.  Engagement at a local level involving all relevant stakeholders will take place this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what representations she has made to the Chancellor of the Exchequer regarding the budgetary position in relation to making public sector pay awards.
answer_text: I met with the Chancellor in London last week along with the First Minister and deputy First Minister. This followed an initial meeting with the Chancellor in July shortly after she came into office. I also met separately with the current Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the end of July.These meetings provided an opportunity to highlight the budget pressures facing the Executive and to the press the Chancellor on the need to make sure the Executive is properly funded to be able to deliver the public services people here need. That included making the case for sufficient funding to be made available for public sector pay.As the Member knows, one of the very first actions the Executive took on its return was to resolve the outstanding public sector pay issues. So, I’m determined to do all I can to make sure that public sector workers here get the pay awards that they are entitled to while at the same time ensuring the Executive delivers a balanced budgetThis will require a recognition from the British Government that our public services are facing a crisis and need investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department plans to (i) deliver climate education in schools; and (ii) decarbonise the schools estate in line with the Executive's net-zero targets.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland Curriculum, climate change and sustainable development are topics covered within the curriculum at every level. At primary it is covered within the ‘World Around Us’ area of learning and at key stage 3, core learning in every subject must be taught in a range of contexts including education for sustainable development.The curriculum at key stage 4 encourages teachers to promote relevance of learning to life and work (including sustainability).Teachers can also contribute to education for sustainable development by providing frequent opportunities, within and beyond the classroom, to help young people develop dispositions and values such as curiosity, concern, respect, tolerance and personal responsibility in relation to a range of environmental and global issues.The Department of Education have also commissioned CCEA to greater embed sustainability and climate change education into the curriculum. This will involve the development of a progression framework and resource hub to draw together up-to-date resources and sources of support for teachers, pupils and young people in green growth and climate change knowledge, skills and understanding. Our existing capital programme is based on design and construction of buildings and extensions to the latest building standards which reflected best practice in heat and insulation and in so doing aided our climate change requirement.Net Zero targets and strategies have been set for the delivery of new net zero school buildings and for the refurbishment of existing buildings (where applicable) to reduce operational energy and minimise the embodied carbon impact of construction works, through energy conservation measures, the use of renewable technologies and low carbon materials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on the review of anti-social behaviour legislation.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 - AQO 747/22-27			ANSWEREarlier this year my Department, in conjunction with DfC, consulted on potential amendments to anti-social behaviour (ASB) legislation, following a cross-departmental, multi-agency review. Following the consultation my Department is working with DfC to address the issue of drinking in public, where there was overwhelming support to amend the legislative framework to tackle this issue. Officials are currently engaged with operational partners and DfC colleagues to finalise proposals in this area. My Department is also leading on consideration of the approach for ASBOs, on which a number of issues were raised via the consultation. A working group, comprising key stakeholders, has been set up to consider the issue and provide advice on how best to proceed. Following conclusion of the working group’s considerations, and upon finalising outstanding issues around drinking in public, it is intended to publish a full Departmental response document, detailing the outcome of the consultation and a proposed way forward.Finally, it is important to note that although this was a joint public consultation with DfC, each Department will now progress any potential legislative amendments arising within their own area of responsibility, and any attendant steps, separately where it can.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria used to select schools to participate in the RAISE Programme; (ii) how the various aspects of that criteria were weighted; (iii) whether the eligibility criteria was based on objective need; (iv) the data used to inform the selection of schools for participation in the RAISE Programme; and (v) the rationale for the omission of nurseries from the programme, given the importance of early intervention as outlined in A Fair Start report.
answer_text: A comprehensive set of child-focused indicators, linked to educational and socio-economic disadvantage, were used to identify priority areas for investment within the RAISE Programme.  The indicators used were:GCSE attainmentAbsence levelsSpecial Educational NeedsFree School Meal EntitlementIncome Deprivation affecting childrenHealth deprivation and disabilityCrime/Anti-Social Behavior IncidentsThe indicators were used to select geographic areas for inclusion in the programme using a two-stage process.Firstly, the seven indicators listed were used for “shortlisting”. A Super Output Area (SOA) was shortlisted if it was in the 30% most disadvantaged in Northern Ireland for 4 or more of the 7 indicators.  Secondly, the shortlisted SOAs were “ranked” using GCSE achievement (5+ GCSEs A*-C including English and maths) with lowest achievement ranking first.This process supported the selection of the most disadvantaged SOAs.  These, in turn, form the basis, or “core”, of the area in which each selected programme locality will operate.  The selection process, as outlined, identified the most disadvantaged SOAs based on the data.  The process was based on objective need with data applied fairly but also recognising that educational disadvantage occurs across Northern Ireland and the programme should support children and young people in each Local Government District (LGD) – achieving geographic coverage and an urban/rural dimension.  Additional shortlisted SOAs, neighbouring the core SOA, were then proposed as an area of influence to form each locality.  Currently, schools with at least 10 pupils living in the localities have been identified to participate in the programme.  A co-design process is underway that will inform the programme design, the community informed interventions and the delivery partners.  The delivery process in each locality will be informed by community partners in that area.   Nursery schools are not excluded from the programme.  The programme will support children and young people from ages 0 to 18.  Officials held initial meetings during June with primary, post-primary and special schools, but plan a series of further engagement opportunities with community stakeholders over the summer and with nursery schools at the earliest opportunity after the summer.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the Communities in Transition programme in West Belfast.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's efforts to improve road safety.
To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the Non-domestic Rating Measures consultation.
answer_text: Over 1,400 responses were received to the consultation on both domestic and non-domestic rating measures which ran from 7 November last year until 13 February. My officials have analysed the responses and provided me with an oral briefing with an overview of the findings. A written report summarising those responses, and an advance copy of that report will be issued shortly to Ministerial colleagues and Finance Committee Members. Following that my officials will be providing a briefing to the Finance Committee on 12 June. The non-domestic rating measures consultation looked at: Industrial Derating, Freight Transport Relief, Non-Domestic Vacant Rating empty property relief and Halls of Residence exemption for halls owned and managed by educational institutions. A limitation of the NIO consultation was that it looked at the singular issue of revenue raising. As an Executive we need to look at a wider range of options than the binary decision to simply remove measures. Decisions will need to align with the strategic priorities of the Executive.Since taking up post I have met with a range of business bodies and organisations. I intend to continue this engagement in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the framework Developing a more strategic approach to 14-19 Education and Training.
answer_text: Oral Question No 13 & 14 – AQO 365 & AQO 366/22-27I recently met with my Ministerial colleague, the Minister for Education to discuss the 14-19 Framework and other shared priorities. During this meeting we reaffirmed our commitment to our departments working collaboratively to support the educational opportunities for all our young people.Given the importance of this work, Minister Givan and I jointly tasked officials with preparing proposals for our consideration to further enhance delivery of the Framework and joint working across the system.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their review of the Together: Building a United Community strategy.
answer_text: The T:BUC Review is ongoing and will be published in due course. The Review process has completed its consultation and research stages.  The analysis and reporting stages are nearing completion.  They seek to comprehensively outline the ten years of achievements under T:BUC.  There is broad consensus that T:BUC has set a good foundation for good relations moving forward.  The headline action model has driven progress across key thematic areas and provided an indication of what works well.There is recognition, however, that our communities and their needs are ever-changing.  We now live in a more diverse population than when T:BUC was launched.   A refreshed approach would be beneficial to ensure that good relations delivery is inclusive to all.The review includes recommendations for next steps, which will be published alongside the full review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the benefits of the Belfast Grand Central Station at Weavers Cross to Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an estimate of when the business case for the construction of All Saints College, Glen Road, will be finalised and provided to stakeholders; and (ii) to detail the projected timeframe for completing the detailed design and planning phases of the project.
answer_text: The business case has been completed and is currently being reviewed before it is issued to the Department of Finance to seek approval.  The review and decision making process is likely to take in the region of two months to complete. Until the preferred option to provide replacement accommodation is approved it is not possible to provide an accurate timeline for completion of detailed design and planning.  However, for a new build project the design phases are likely to take in the region of two years to complete following business case approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work the Department is undertaking to assess the potential impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, within its areas of responsibility.
answer_text: The Department has not identified any potential impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, within its areas of responsibility. However, the Department continues to engage with colleagues in relevant departments, both in Northern Ireland and Great Britain to ensure that appropriate consideration and action is undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to list the legislation, relating to areas of responsibility of the Department, that is affected by the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.
answer_text: The Department has not identified any legislation, relating to areas of responsibility of the Department, that is affected by the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when will NI Water connect the housing development on 35 Ballymaconaghy Road, Belfast to the sewerage system.
answer_text: The management of sewerage infrastructure at Ballymaconaghy Road, Belfast and across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. I have been informed by NI Water that you have raised this issue with them directly and they are preparing a response to your query.
